body_txt: When you get down to it, there's not much difference between what men and women can do. Even if on average one gender or sex is more suited to the role, as long as it doesn't involve reproduction, there will be exceptions to the rule. And sometimes even then . Even with physical appearance, if nudity isn't required, there are some people who can convincingly pass themselves off as the other gender. And this isn't even getting into transgender people. Not so in fiction, where your access to the supernatural powers or ability to use the Applied Phlebotinum may depend on your gender. If so, there are rarely any exceptions to this rule. Although with this being said, woman do tend to have a leg up, if this trope is used/in effect. This is especially true, if they hail from an harem anime/manga ( for obvious or not so obvious reasons ) to justify an Improbably Female Cast or an feminist-oriented work. This trope doesn't apply to cultural gender divides, only to times when certain magic or supernatural powers are restricted to one gender. In the case of magic, sometimes the sexes will both have access to it but either use it in a fundamentally different way or one gender having far more than the other (often to the point where the weaker gender can´t even use it or is overall useless). This, in case for the first, often results in Unequal Rites , as they disagree over which system is better. If only one gender has access to it however, than it will result in a Sliding Scale of Gender Inequality with (just as stated above) the woman being usually favourized. This trope is a common way of making a character a Supernaturally Validated Trans Person , and, if not addressed by the work, an excellent way to make a fandom descend into (potentially) incessant debates about how an intersex, nonbinary, genderfluid or transgender person would interact with such a system. Might be hand-waved by Bizarre Sexual Dimorphism . Compare Gender-Restricted Gear , mostly found in Video Games . Super-Trope to Magic Is Feminine . Compare Gender Incompetence . See also Racially Exclusive Magic , which could be considered the race-based counterpart.
